dialogue.utterance: Unit 77, respond to Brook Rd and School St for a vehicle fire and entrapment.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What brought you to call 911?
I heard a crash. When I got to the scene, the car was on its roof and on fire. The driver was still inside.
Is the patient responding-eye movements, verbal sounds, or movement at all?
No, he doesn't appear to be moving or breathing from a distance.
His eyes are open, but they appear milky and show no signs of awareness right now.
There are no motor responses observed at the scene.
Airway is semi-patent, but compromised due to heavy soot and inhalation risks.
Given soot presence and shallow respirations, we will secure the airway with intubation.
His pulse is at 144 BPM, central pulses are bounding.
Breathing is labored and shallow. Checking for Tachypnea.
Respirations are at 63 RPM, but reduced to 46 on recheck-tachypnea noted.
Oxygen saturation cannot be obtained due to complications from burn injuries.
Central pulses are strong, and skin appears hot and charred, but no peripheral pulses noted.
Blood pressure is reading 105 over 69.
Blood pressure trends lower to 96 over 44 now. We must keep a close eye on this.
Glasgow Coma Scale of 3-no eye, verbal, or motor response.
Administering high-flow oxygen via endotracheal tube at 25 LPM to address hypoxia risk.
EKG showing sinus tachycardia with a heart rate of 144.
Full- and partial-thickness burns noted across more than 75% of the body surface.
Soot in the nose and mouth, milky eyes, and partial-thickness burn to the neck.
Post-intubation, full-thickness chest burns confirmed; lungs show rhonchi.
Abdomen has extensive burns without signs of rigidity or guarding.
Full-thickness burns to extremities and lower back-minimal distal perfusion.
Do you know anything about the patient's medical history or current medications?
I do not-no prior medical knowledge witnessed here.
What did you see immediately before the accident occurred?
Heard a crash and found the car flipped. He was inside, which prompted the 911 call.
Because he is unresponsive, pain is estimated at 0 based on altered mental status.
Airway secured-7.5 ET tube placed at 23", with chest rise and misting.
Rechecking-heart rate is 150-significantly elevated.
Tachypnea continues at 46 respirations per minute.
His blood pressure has significantly dropped to 96 over 44.
IO access established in the left humeral head-controls fluid delivery as per protocol.
Unable to check glucose-burn damage and lack of reliable vascular access.
4-lead ECG applied for cardiac monitoring during continued evaluation.
Reassessing vital signs: 150, 46, 96 over 44-stable for now.
Airway stable. IO line confirms patency and delivers fluids per protocol.
Transport destination is VCU Health Systems due to their burn care specialty.
Patient is fully covered with a burn sheet and strapped into the stretcher.
Transporting with lights and sirens for rapid arrival to the trauma center.
This call involves fire entrapment from a rollover MVC. Heat and carbon exposure.
Normal saline bolus infused via IO access to support hemodynamics during transport.
Airway secured with ETT remains stable. Monitoring with ongoing ECG.
EKG remained in sinus tachycardia with steady workload.
Rechecking equal chest rise post-intubation and clean rhonchi in both lungs.
No palpable asymmetry or crepitation at the pelvis.
No guarding or rigidity detected despite complete area burn coverage.
Rechecking airway for presence of soot and waveform confirmation post-ECC entry.
No signs of consciousness or spontaneous respiratory return noted.
Stabilizing vitals: 144 BPM, 63 RPM, 105/69-minimal improvement from earlier.
Updated vitals at 150 BPM, 46 RPM, 96/44-showing only tempo change.
Monitoring IO infusions and assessing perfusion visually to aid situational awareness.
Re-checking skin-no perfusion to extremities, consolidated sloughing noted.
Unable to establish last oral intake or food activity information due to unconsciousness.
Suggesting improved traffic lanes for faster VCU response as airway is secured.
Opting for Charles Street to avoid obstructions for optimal transit access.
Fire suppression complete and patient extracted from scene.
Let me confirm again: how long had the crash occurred before the 911 call?
Crash was heard directly-911 called within five minutes max.
Trauma alert confirmed at VCU. Their ER team will be prepared upon arrival.
Reporting a critical patient to VCU: 37-year-old male, entrapment, thermal burns, and MVC.
Checking IO flush and fluid continuity for proper transport protocol maintenance.
Pulse continuation is pending-maintaining standard ventilation protocol until CCU.
Eye clarity remains uniformly opalescent, with pupil unavailability for response checks.
Staging at VCU ground per hospital directive for immediate ED care.
ED on standby with arrival time deadline in 12 minutes.
VCU chosen as a center with trauma and burn care specialties active on call.
Reassessing-still sloughing noted on right hand with flame pattern burn coverage.
Must note facial and neck burns, airway compromise likely is expected.
Confirming lane availability via dispatch for timely medical transport.
Summarizing current status for VCU handover without embellishment.
Reconfirming vitals-150 BPM, 46 BPM, BP 96 / 44 for log update.
EKG continues as sinus tachycardia, steady without interval change.
Extremities view only shows char and slough across full surface burn coverage.
Ensure patient is elevated enough to help support positioning and breathing needs.
Double-checking baseline duties to confirm continuity into medical logs.
Alert remains active with VCU-query on ED status for readiness visuals.
All mandated procedures under protocol: intubation, IO, EKG for transport endorsed status.
Vital signs remain stable-no immediate life-threatening concerns in transit.
Compressing and logbook entries completed, compliant with real-time and policy evidence.
Robust build observed, but no systemic support for fluids. Burn status notes for VCU.
Consider prior seating orientation to avoid injury due to body positioning upon ED shift.
Count starts at EVAC from vehicle-fires, entry, application series.
Alright, understand to proceed with patient and gear mobilization.
PT Form finalization for ER team to receive and maintain incident documentation.
Tracking departure sequence timestamp against projected ED time.
CO2 waveform still current and overall detection within standard safezone.
Secured all backup essentials-vehicle storage confirmed.
Identifying severely hindered lane alternatives from dispatch mapping.
Inquiry against sector mapping for known lane usage during this call.
Conference with onlookers and specialists complete-all pertinent field reports integrated.
No response pending from ED-confirming approximated arrival window here.
All procedures and vitals recorded as per protocol. Completion truth-no go further.
Assuming readiness clear to exit-All systems greening for VCU transport start.
